source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
objectives, curing state and society of its ‘big and small ills’23 and using tech-
nology for ‘social management’.24 This paper only mentions internal aims
and eﬀects of the SCS when they are relevant to the external impact and
transnational law as a political project. The research questions thus focus
on the implications for those outside the national borders of the PRC.
While this is a legitimate focus, and one that is less researched than the
internal impact, it is not an analysis of the SCS in its entirety but rather
asks the question of how far the CSCS is an example of transnational law
as a political project with eﬀects beyond the national.25
   Furthermore, the SCS is a system in progress as well as a vast enterprise.
The SCS, as envisioned in the Planning Outline, is not yet fully implemented
and has not yet reached the status of a uniﬁed, universal and nation-wide
system. At the same time, the aim of assessing the geopolitical and transna-
tional regulatory relevance requires to make predictions. Hence, some parts
of the argument are based on the likely development of the system as
opposed to certainty. However, the analysis rests on existing example
systems which are either local systems of speciﬁc regions or cities or
which are based on commercial practice (eg, Sesame Credit) or which are
nation-wide systems which have a more restricted scope (eg, blacklists) as
well as the aims and mechanisms stated in the Planning Outline of the
State Council.26 Nevertheless, most research on the SCS builds on the
interpretation of text; empirical research might come to diﬀerent conclusions
on the factual impact of the SCS.
